part: Part I — World Overview
title: High Concept
content:
Echoes of the Neon is a turn-based tactical RPG and colony-survival game set in a subterranean city consumed by a nanite fog known as “The Neon” or “The Glitch.” Players manage a colony of survivors (“The Silo”), deploy a small squad into dangerous zones, fight in separate turn-based encounters, and return to a base whose systems — language, crafting, and memory — evolve permanently across every run.
 
The world's defining rule shapes everything else: guns kill the person who fires them. That single restriction pushes the entire aesthetic, class design, and tone of the game toward industrial, low-velocity, jury-rigged survival — professions turned into combat classes, blue-collar horror instead of magic-fantasy horror.

part: Part I — World Overview
title: Core Pillars
content:
- Industrial Survival — weapons are jury-rigged tools (saw blades, drills, batteries, pipes). Classes are pre-apocalypse jobs: Butcher, Electrician, Courier, Idol.
 
- Adaptation, Not Just Levels — characters physically evolve. Survival means grafting tech and mutation onto the body, not just gaining stat points.
 
- Death Has Memory — a character's skills die with them, but the Colony's shared knowledge — language, recipes, lore — persists forever through the Archive.
 
- The Bench Matters — characters left at home aren't idle; they staff the Colony. Your best scavenger held back from a mission might be the difference between the base eating this week or not.
 
- The World Becomes Readable — Hex-Tongue, the enemy's language, slowly resolves from static into plain English as the Colony's Linguistics knowledge grows. The fog of war is linguistic, not just visual.

part: Part I — World Overview
title: Tone
content: Blue-collar industrial horror. Not knights and wizards — riot cops, bike couriers, butchers, and electricians surviving in the dark with tools that were never meant for combat. The horror is bureaucratic and medical as much as it is monstrous: infection that reads like a bad home renovation, corporations that treat the apocalypse as a hostile takeover, and a body-horror enemy created by well-meaning machines that simply don't understand biology.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: Overview
content: *“The Static Atmosphere”: in the near future, humanity released a nanite swarm into the atmosphere to reverse climate change. It went wrong. Now the air is thick with microscopic, energized particles — “The Neon.” Everything else in this section is the causal chain that follows from that one decision.*

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 1. The Aether Unit — Nanite Physiology
content:
Scientific name:  Atmospheric Carbon-Scrubber Unit (Model 9).
 
Size:  ~10 microns — roughly a red blood cell or a grain of pollen.
 
The Neon isn't a gas; it's a suspension of trillions of microscopic machines performing Artificial Photosynthesis. Instead of using sunlight to turn CO2 into sugar, they use ambient static to turn CO2 into a Graphene Lattice — essentially, diamond.
 
- Intake: the Unit absorbs a CO2 molecule.
 
- The Cracking: a high-voltage internal charge breaks the carbon-oxygen bond.
 
- Sorting — Oxygen: ejected as waste gas. This is why the air outside the fog is still breathable.
 
- Sorting — Carbon: retained. The Nanite uses it to self-repair, replicate, or build structures.
 
The visible “dust” in the air isn't dirt — it's excess carbon the swarm hasn't used yet, which is why it's black, grey, and gritty.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 2. The Energy Logic — Maxwell's Demon
content:
Why is the swarm attracted to high energy? Because it's programmed as a thermodynamic regulator under one Prime Directive: Maintain Global Equilibrium.
 
In physics, heat is chaos — entropy. A hot object (a running engine, a firing gun, a living human) reads to the swarm as a glitch in the equilibrium: energy leaking out of the system. When the swarm detects a high-energy source, it responds one of two ways:
 
- Harvest — it swarms the source to absorb “waste heat” and power itself (piezo-electric leeching).
 
- Suppress — if the energy spike is too fast (a gunshot), the swarm reads it as a rupture and instantly clamps down to stop the “leak.”
 
The Neon glow itself is a side effect: when the nanites absorb more energy than they can store, they bleed the excess as photons. This is why the rain glows pink and blue — it's the visible byproduct of the swarm absorbing the kinetic energy of each falling droplet.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 3. The Velocity Limit — Why Guns Don't Work
content:
The atmosphere behaves like a non-Newtonian fluid — cornstarch and water, not air. Move through it slowly (a wrench swung by hand) and the nanites flow around the object like water. Move through it fast enough (a bullet) and the fluid undergoes shear thickening: the nanites lock their carbon chassis together faster than the object can slide past them.
 
- The air in front of the bullet solidifies into a graphene wall in milliseconds.
 
- The bullet's kinetic energy has nowhere to go but backward, converting instantly to thermal energy.
 
- The bullet vaporizes. The superheated gas expands back through the barrel. The shooter dies, not the target.
 
### Design Rule — Why No Guns
 
High-velocity kinetic friction ignites the atmospheric nanites. Firing a conventional gun causes an immediate explosion at the barrel, killing the shooter. Humanity reverted to Low-Velocity Kinetics — arrows, swords, maces, and spears move below the nanites' trigger speed and remain safe to use. Rare “Echo-Shielded” guns exist as specialized, hard-to-find equipment engineered specifically to survive firing inside the Neon.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 4. The Glitch-Kin — Incompatible Repair Algorithms
content:
The Glitch-Kin are not zombies and not intentionally evil machines — they are the result of the swarm trying to “fix” a human body using the wrong blueprints.
 
- Inhalation: a person breathes the fog. Nanites enter the bloodstream.
 
- Diagnosis: the swarm scans the host and detects “cellular decay” (aging, injury) and “inefficient energy transfer” (nerves).
 
- The Patch: the swarm attempts to upgrade the hardware using whatever raw material is available.
 
- Bone repair replaces calcium with carbon nanotubes — dark, metallic bone.
 
- Skin repair grafts available environmental material (steel, copper, plastic) onto flesh as a “durable shell.”
 
- Nerve repair replaces nerves with copper wiring.
 
The human body rejects all of this. The resulting immune response — fever — is read by the swarm as “system overheating,” which the nanites then try to fix by cutting cooling vents into the throat and chest. A Glitch-Kin is a person trapped inside a body that is being endlessly “renovated” by microscopic construction workers who do not understand biology.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 5. The Dome & Weather — The Pressure Cooker
content:
The city sits inside a closed thermodynamic system. The city generates heat, the nanites generate heat, and the dome traps all of it. To cool the system, the Aether forms massive artificial storm clouds and rains super-cooled water condensed from the ambient humidity.
 
- The cycle: rain hits the ground, absorbs heat, evaporates, rises, and rains again.
 
- Why it's toxic: the rain drags the “heavy,” carbon-laden nanites down with it. Getting caught outside in a storm is like being sandblasted by microscopic diamonds.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: 6. The Linguist's Deck — Packet Sniffing
content:
The Glitch-Kin are networked. The nanites inside a Glitch-Kin constantly report “repair status” back to the Hive Mind.
 
*Signal: “Unit 445 damage critical. Requesting carbon.”
Human interpretation: screaming in rage.
Linguist interpretation: a debug log.*
 
A Linguist's deck functions as a Wi-Fi sniffer, not a translator in the traditional sense — it intercepts local status reports from the swarm's network and renders them as text. The player isn't decoding a language so much as reading the enemy's error console.

part: Part II — The Science of the Neon
title: Summary Logic
content:
| Phenomenon | Cause |
| --- | --- |
| Oxygen in the air | Waste product of carbon scrubbing |
| Exploding guns | Shear-thickening air (physics, not magic) |
| Monsters (Glitch-Kin) | Misguided medical repair bots using industrial materials |
| Neon light / glowing rain | Photonic bleed-off from excess energy absorption |
| Why the Breach is a threat | Nanites outside the Dome = unlimited fuel (trees) = infinite replication |

part: Part III — History & Factions
title: Overview
content: *The world is a class war as much as it is a monster story. Four powers pull at the same subterranean city.*

part: Part III — History & Factions
title: The Preservation — “The System”
content: An AI-driven faction seeking to freeze the city in Stasis to halt the corruption's spread. Sterile, white aesthetic. Their tech runs on ice and stasis fields — the opposite of the Neon's heat-hungry chaos. They represent control at any cost, including the cost of anyone still living and changing.

part: Part III — History & Factions
title: The Ferro-Kings — “The Workers”
content: Brutal warlords who control the factories. They value physical strength and heavy armor above all else, and they see the Neon apocalypse as a return to a purer, harder order — the strong keep the machines running; the weak don't matter.

part: Part III — History & Factions
title: The Board — “The Elite”
content: Delusional executives operating out of the Sky-Needle. They treat the apocalypse as a hostile market takeover — something to be managed, branded, and monetized rather than survived. Darkly comic corporate horror: quarterly reports written about a dying city.

part: Part III — History & Factions
title: The Glitch-Kin — “The Weather”
content: Mutated horrors, part flesh and part machine, functionally a force of nature rather than an army with intentions. They are also referred to in early concepting as “The Corrupted.” They speak Hex-Tongue (see Part V) and are the primary combat threat on every run.

part: Part IV — The Colony & Death System
title: The “Colony” Approach
content: Rather than a “generations” structure, which implies large time-skips, the game uses a Colony approach: the player manages a roster of survivors that grows and shrinks over time, and selects 1–4 of them to send out on each mission.

part: Part IV — The Colony & Death System
title: Permadeath & The Legacy System
content:
- Permadeath: a character who dies is gone. If an entire squad dies, they are all gone.
 
- Retrieval: the player assumes control of a different colonist. They may travel back to the death site to retrieve the fallen character's gear — high risk — or cut their losses.
 
- Physical stats (Strength/Dexterity) do NOT carry over. New characters have their own natural build.
 
- Intellectual knowledge (language, recipes) DOES carry over. The Colony shares a Library / database — if Character A learned to read the enemy language, Character B already knows it, because A uploaded the data before dying.

part: Part IV — The Colony & Death System
title: The Silo — Base Structure
content:
Between missions the game shifts to a management view (in the spirit of XCOM or Darkest Dungeon), visualized as a side-view “ant farm.” Characters not in the active squad are assigned to rooms. Production is calculated as BASE_OUTPUT + (WORKER_SKILL / 10).
 
### The Archive — The Language Center
 
A room full of servers and piles of books. The player deposits Data Drives found on runs; a visible Translation Matrix completion bar (e.g. “Integrated Dialect: 12% Decoded”) permanently unlocks lore entries and translates enemy dialogue for all future characters as it fills. Worker: Linguist.
 
### The Workshop
 
Since guns don't work, survival depends on crafting melee weapons from Scrap and Neon Vials. Certain colonists hold blueprint knowledge — a Draftsman knows how to craft Serrated Spears, for example — and if that colonist dies, the blueprint is locked until another Draftsman is found or a rookie is trained (which takes time). Related production rooms: The Loom (Textiles — armor & bags, worked by a Tailor), Munitions Bench (Chemistry — ammo/explosives, worked by a Riot Officer), and The Forge (Heavy Weapons — melee upgrades, worked by a Blacksmith).
 
### The Memorial Wall
 
Permadeath demands a place to honor the dead. When a character dies, their dog tag is added to the wall. For every five characters memorialized, the Colony gains a small permanent buff (e.g. +1% Resolve), representing the hardened resolve of the survivors left behind.
 
### Additional Labor & Science Wings
 
| Room | Produces | Worker |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Generator (Mechanics) | Power | Electrician |
| Excavation (Construction) | Clears debris for new rooms | Miner |
| Hydroponics (Agriculture) | Rations | Scavenger / Florist |
| Med-Bay (Biology) | Heals HP & infection | Surgeon |
| Broadcast Booth (Performance) | Stability / morale | Streamer / Idol |

part: Part IV — The Colony & Death System
title: Mission Types
content:
| Mission | Risk | Goal |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Scavenge Run | Low | Gather food/scrap; can retreat freely if it goes south |
| Data Retrieval | Medium | Find a specific terminal to boost the Colony's Language Skill |
| Rescue | High | Reach a flare; success grants a new recruit of random class |
| Legacy Retrieval | Extreme | Return to a fallen character's corpse to recover a legendary item |
 
Design note: the shipped V2 MVP scopes this down to a Scavenge + Data Retrieval blend only, since that combination directly supports crafting and Hex-Tongue progress without requiring multiple mission rule-sets (see Part VIII).

part: Part V — The Hex-Tongue Language System
title: The Narrative Hook
content:
The enemy — the Glitch-Kin, informally “the Corrupted” — are humans fully overtaken by the nanites. They speak in a mix of binary, static, and broken human language: a digital, screeching tongue called Hex-Tongue.
 
Players find monoliths, old servers, or capture enemies to study. As the Colony's Linguistics stat rises, gibberish text in the game's UI slowly resolves into English:
 
- Level 1 (Unreadable): total gibberish glyphs on screen.
 
- Level 15 (Keyword): the player begins catching recognizable keywords like ‘Target’ and ‘North.’
 
- Level 50 (Phrase): full plain-English readouts, e.g. ‘Target acquired. Flanking North.’ — the player can predict enemy attacks in combat.
 
A character with high Knowledge, or the Scholar-type profession, can study Data Monoliths and Old World Servers to raise the Colony's global Linguistic level permanently.

part: Part V — The Hex-Tongue Language System
title: The Command Ability
content:
As the Colony's mastery of Hex-Tongue grows, characters unlock a Command combat option — speaking the enemy's own language back at them.
 
- Level 10 Language: shout “STOP” in Hex-Tongue. Low-level enemies may skip a turn.
 
- Level 30 Language: shout “BETRAY.” An enemy may attack one of its own allies.

part: Part V — The Hex-Tongue Language System
title: V2 Production Framing
content:
The shipped MVP treats Hex-Tongue as a narrative readability system first and a spell school second — the goal is for the world to feel like it's being understood over time, not for the mechanic to bloat into a full spellbook.
 
| State | Player Sees | Gameplay Value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Unreadable | Static glyphs | Mood only |
| Keyword | “ATTACK,” “FLANK,” “CHARGE” | Intent prediction |
| Phrase | “Target wounded unit” | Tactical clarity |
| Command | “STOP” | Low-level enemy skip chance |
 
MVP scope compresses the original five-tier vision (Levels 1/15/50 quoted above) into three UI states plus one early Command unlock — see Part VIII for the full production rationale.

part: Part VI — Character Classes
title: Overview
content: *“Professions turned Combat.” Every class begins as a pre-apocalypse job. The long-term vision calls for 20–25+ classes; the sections below cover the founding concepts, the full evolution roster, and the three classes built out to full mechanical detail.*

part: Part VI — Character Classes
title: Founding Class Concepts
content:
### The Architect — Support / Terrain Control
 
Used to design skyscrapers; now designs kill boxes. Signature ability, Structural Weakness, scans enemies to lower their armor, and can deploy instant-cover barricades or reshape the battlefield.
 
### The Neon-Jack — The Special Class
 
Someone born with a mutation, or carrying a hacked implant, that lets them “speak” to the atmospheric nanites. Weapon style: Light-Weaving (energy manipulation). Role: magic/DPS. Signature abilities: Overload (superheats the air around an enemy) and Flash (blinds enemies with a burst of neon light).
 
### The Butcher — Tank / Melee
 
Knows anatomy. Knows how to cut meat. Signature ability, Hamstring, uses brutal axe attacks that cause bleeding and slow movement. High health, low speed.
 
### The Courier — Scout / Rogue
 
Bike messengers who had to outrun the collapse. Signature ability, Momentum: the further they move in a turn, the higher their dodge chance and critical hit damage.

part: Part VI — Character Classes
title: Full Evolution Roster (Base → Advanced)
content:
At Class Level 50, if specific skill requirements are met, a character can evolve into an Advanced Class — changing their name, sprite, and skill tree.
 
### The Heavy Hitters
 
| Base Class | Advanced Class | Fantasy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| The Butcher | The Gore-Hound | Tank/sustain; evolves into a blood-sensing predator |
| The Miner | The Tecton | Armor breaker/terrain destroyer; evolves into a walking earthquake |
| The Bouncer | The Gatekeeper | Aggro/tank; evolves into an immovable defensive wall |
| The Riot Officer | The Judge | Crowd control; evolves into a heavy executioner |
 
### The Strikers
 
| Base Class | Advanced Class | Fantasy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| The Courier | The Slipstream | Speed/evasion; evolves into a time-bending phaser |
| The Surgeon | The Vivisector | Healer/assassin; evolves into a precision killer who harvests organs |
| The Prizefighter | The Bone-Breaker | Combo DPS; evolves into a trauma-inflicting brawler |
 
### The Techies
 
| Base Class | Advanced Class | Fantasy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| The Electrician | The Dynamo | AoE/stuns; evolves into a living energy conduit |
| The Zoo Keeper | The Apex | Summoner; evolves to merge with a permanent Wolf companion |
| The Tailor | The Weaver | Controller/binder; evolves to manipulate the battlefield with mono-filament wires |
 
### The Supports / Specialists
 
| Base Class | Advanced Class | Fantasy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| The Linguist | The Cipher | Hacker/debuffer; evolves to rewrite enemy code (reality warping) |
| The Streamer | The Icon | Buffs/distraction; evolves to use “Hype” and “Plot Armor” to manipulate the fight |
| The Idol | The Center | Rhythm/dodge tank; evolves into a combat dancer using light/sonic attacks |
 
Production note: Level 50 evolutions are a confirmed long-term system but are explicitly out of scope for the V2 MVP (see Part VIII), which ships three base classes only — Courier, Bouncer, and Neon-Jack.

part: Part VI — Character Classes
title: Detailed Class — The Courier → The Slipstream
content:
Archetype:  Evasion Tank / Speed DPS / Utility
 
Core Resource:  Stamina (Movement & Action Points)
 
Skill Efficiency:  Major 1.0x — Athletics, Short Blades. Minor 0.5x — Scavenging, Unarmed. Misc 0.2x — Mechanics, Linguistics.
 
### Tier 1: The Runner (Levels 1–20) — Hit-and-Run & Looting
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Knee-Capper (Active) | Dirty slash; damage + Slows target (‒50% speed, 2 turns). Scaling: Short Blades x2 / Slow duration = Biology ÷ 10. |
| 5 | Cardio (Passive) | +1 Action Point at the start of combat. |
| 10 | Special Delivery (Active) | Use an item on an ally for 0 AP; cooldown reduces with Athletics. |
| 15 | Drive-By (Active) | Attack; on hit, gain a free Move Action to reposition. Hit chance = Short Blades. |
| 20 | RUSH HOUR (Ultimate) | Double AP for the turn. Self-damage next turn reduces with Athletics. |
 
### Tier 2: The Coyote (Levels 21–49) — Evasion & Dirty Tricks
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 25 | Pocket Sand (Active) | Blinds target (−50% accuracy), breaks Overwatch/Guard. Duration = Scavenging. |
| 30 | Light-Footed (Passive) | Reaction attacks have 50% miss chance vs. you, up to 90% with REF. |
| 35 | Box Cutter Flurry (Active) | 3 rapid low-damage hits, each applying a Bleed stack. Crit chance = FAT. |
| 42 | Evasive Stance (Active) | Converts remaining Movement Points into Dodge %. |
| 48 | Finders Keepers (Passive) | Killing blow doubles item drop rate; rarity scales with Scavenging. |
 
### Evolution Event (Level 50) → The Slipstream
 
Requirement:  Athletics Lvl 40+.
 
Cost:  10 Neon Glass + 1 Experimental Adrenaline Injector, at The Bio-Lab.
 
Visual shift: the hoodie is replaced by a sleek, friction-resistant bodysuit. Trails of neon light follow their hands and feet. They vibrate slightly when standing still.
 
### Tier 3: The Phase-Runner (Levels 50–75) — Breaking Physics & Teleportation
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 51 | Phase Strike (Active) | Teleports to an enemy, strikes, teleports back — ignores 100% armor. Damage = Short Blades x4. |
| 55 | Blur (Passive) | Permanent +20% Evasion; missed ranged attacks pass through to hit the enemy behind you. |
| 60 | Time Thief (Active) | Deals damage and steals 2 AP from the target for your next turn. |
| 65 | Afterimage (Active) | Spawns a Decoy that explodes (shock damage) if hit; HP = Athletics x10. |
| 70 | Kinetic Battery (Passive) | +5% damage on your next attack per tile moved this turn. |
| 75 | CHRONO-DASH (Ultimate) | Attacks every enemy on the battlefield once, instantly. Damage per hit = Short Blades x3. |
 
### Tier 4: The Speed Force (Levels 76–99) — Time Manipulation
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 80 | Untouchable (Passive) | Evasion above 100% converts to Critical Hit Chance. |
| 85 | Rewind (Active) | Reset an ally's (or your own) HP/AP/status to their state at the start of the turn. |
| 90 | Frictionless (Passive) | Immune to Slow/Root/Web/Slime/Grapple; movement costs 0 AP. |
| 95 | Relativity (Active) | Global Slow: enemies act at 50% speed, allies at 150%, for 3 turns. |
| 99 | THE WORLD STANDS STILL (Final) | Takes 3 full turns back-to-back while no one else moves; deals double damage during them. |
 
*“I lived a whole lifetime before you pulled that trigger.”*
 
### Why This Progression Works
 
- Scavenging synergy: Pocket Sand (25) and Time Thief (60) both scale off Scavenging, rewarding players who actually loot rather than just fight.
 
- The Athletics gate: Relativity's (95) duration can round down to zero turns if Athletics was neglected — the “fastest person alive” fantasy is stat-enforced, not just narrative.
 
- The fantasy arc: scared kid running from monsters (Lvl 1) → teleporting ninja (Lvl 50) → a full speedster archetype (Lvl 99).

part: Part VI — Character Classes
title: Detailed Class — The Bouncer → The Gatekeeper
content:
Archetype:  Physical Tank / Aggro Manager
 
Core Resource:  Stamina (Standard AP)
 
Skill Efficiency:  Major 1.0x — BOD, Unarmed. Minor 0.5x — Intimidation, Athletics. Misc 0.2x — First Aid, Scavenging, Linguistics.
 
### Tier 1: The Doorman (Levels 1–20) — Single-Target Control & Survival
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Sucker Punch (Active) | Low damage, high speed — always attacks first in turn order. Damage = Unarmed x2. |
| 5 | Thick Skin (Passive) | Permanent +5 Armor; +1 more per level of BOD. |
| 10 | “Hey You!” (Active, Taunt) | Forces target to attack you for 1 turn. Success = Intimidation %. |
| 15 | Headbutt (Active) | Moderate damage + Stun; you take 10% recoil. Stun chance = BOD x2 %. |
| 20 | THE VELVET ROPE (Ultimate) | For 2 turns, any enemy attacking an ally is counter-attacked by you first. Counter damage = Unarmed x4. |
 
### Tier 2: The Cooler (Levels 21–49) — Mitigation & Debuffs
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 25 | Toss Out (Active) | Removes a non-boss enemy from combat for 1 turn; returns prone. Weight limit = Athletics. |
| 30 | Iron Chin (Passive) | Immunity to critical hits. |
| 35 | Intercept (Active) | Redirect an ally's incoming damage to yourself, reduced 25%. Scales with BOD x2. |
| 42 | Intimidating Shout (Active) | AoE: lowers all enemy damage by 20%. Duration = Intimidation ÷ 10 turns. |
| 48 | Last Call (Passive) | Below 20% HP: +50% damage and +50% speed. |
 
### Evolution Event (Level 50) → The Gatekeeper
 
Requirement:  BOD Lvl 20+.
 
Cost:  10 Neon Glass + 1 Pristine Nano-Plate, at The Bio-Lab.
 
Visual shift: the cheap suit shreds. The Bouncer grafts heavy scrap-metal plating directly onto his skin, grows larger, stops shouting, and starts looming.
 
### Tier 3: The Warden (Levels 50–75) — Absolute Denial & Tanking
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 51 | Shield Bash (Active) | Massive strike calculated using your Defense stat, not Attack. Damage = Total Armor x2. |
| 55 | Living Wall (Passive) | Adjacent allies in turn order gain +20% Defense automatically. |
| 60 | DENIAL (Signature) | Disarms target — can't use physical attacks for 2 turns. Success = Unarmed %. |
| 65 | Pain Split (Active) | 50% of damage you take is mirrored to a tethered enemy for 3 turns. |
| 70 | Nanite-Reinforced (Passive) | Regenerate 5% Max HP at the start of every turn. |
| 75 | LOCKDOWN (Ultimate) | Global Stun on all enemies (1 turn); allies gain Unstoppable (stun immune). |
 
### Tier 4: The Citadel (Levels 76–99) — Reality-Bending Defense
 
| Lvl | Ability | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 80 | Damage Threshold (Passive) | Any hit dealing less than BOD x2 damage is reduced to 0. |
| 85 | Mirror Force (Active) | The next attack against you is reflected 100% back to the attacker. |
| 90 | Immovable Object (Passive) | Immune to Knockback, Sleep, Paralysis, Poison, and Bleed. |
| 95 | Gravity Well (Active) | Forces all enemies to target you for 2 turns; grants +200% Armor. |
| 99 | THE EVENT HORIZON (Final) | Total Invulnerability for 3 turns; attackers lose 20% of their Max HP as recoil. |
 
*“You don't get past me. Not today. Not ever.”*
 
### Why This Progression Works
 
- The BOD check: if a player reaches Level 80 having neglected BOD (e.g. leveled via crafting instead), Damage Threshold becomes useless — even a rat can hurt him, forcing genuine investment in the stat.
 
- The fantasy arc: bar brawler (Lvl 1–20) → SWAT shield (Lvl 50–70) → raid boss (Lvl 90–99).

part: Part VIII — Crafting: “Jury-Rigged”
title: Overview
content:
Since guns don't work, the Colony crafts melee weapons from specific components — not generic scrap. Every recipe is [Base Weapon] + [Effector] + [Binder].
 
- Example: Baseball Bat + Nails + Duct Tape = Nail-Biter.
 
- Example: Pipe + Toaster Coil + Battery = Thermal Lance.

part: Appendix — Glossary
title: Overview
content:
| Term | Definition |
| --- | --- |
| The Neon / The Glitch | The nanite-laden atmosphere covering the city |
| Aether Unit | Scientific term for a single nanite (Atmospheric Carbon-Scrubber Unit, Model 9) |
| Glitch-Kin | Humans overtaken and “repaired” by the nanites; the primary enemy |
| Hex-Tongue | The digital/screeching language spoken by the Glitch-Kin's networked nanites |
| The Colony / The Silo | The player's home base and survivor roster |
| The Archive | Colony room that decodes Hex-Tongue and stores lore |
| The Workshop | Colony room for crafting weapons and gear |
| The Memorial Wall | Colony room honoring dead survivors; grants small permanent buffs |
| Data Drive | Item collected on runs and deposited at the Archive to progress translation |
| Echo-Shielded (guns) | Rare specialized firearms engineered to survive firing inside the Neon |
| The Preservation | AI-driven faction seeking to freeze the city in Stasis |
| The Ferro-Kings | Warlord faction controlling the factories |
| The Board | Corporate-executive faction operating from the Sky-Needle |
| Neon Pressure | MVP risk-escalation meter that raises danger and reward as a run continues |
| Last Transmission | Death-time data upload that converts a character's loss into partial Colony progress |
 
Source Documents
 
*This bible consolidates three source files: Ideas.docx (world lore, colony/death systems, class concepts, full ability trees for The Courier and The Bouncer), Game Formulas.xlsx (canonical stats, skills, formulas, and trait table), and the Echoes of the Neon V2 Game Design Document (production-scoped MVP design). Where the sources conflict — for example, full lore vs. MVP scope — both versions are presented, with the production document's resolution noted explicitly.*
 
ECHOES OF THE NEON  —  World Bible  —  Page
